title: Spreadsheets, the finale
deck: The math is done. Now we automate it. A spreadsheet is a giant grid of functions, and after Sections 1.1 through 1.5 we already know how to read every one of them.
objectives: Use spreadsheet applications: functions, cell references, and basic graphing.

content.text: Imagine going back to the study-abroad savings model from Section 1.5: $200 to start, $75 each week, reaching the deposit goal around week 22. A reasonable next question is, “What if I could only save $60 a week instead of $75?” A second reasonable question is, “What if my starting balance were $150?” A third is, “What if I want to see every week between now and graduation laid out in a table?” Each of those questions is short to ask, and painful to answer with pencil and paper.

Spreadsheets exist for exactly that pain. Excel does not replace the mathematics of the previous five sections; it scales them. We write the formula once, change a single input, and watch every dependent prediction update automatically. This section introduces the small set of moves, cell addresses, the equals sign, and two friendly built-in functions, that turn the slope-intercept work of Section 1.5 into a real tool.
1.6.1  Cells, addresses, and formulas

A spreadsheet is a grid of rectangles called cells. Columns are labeled with letters (A, B, C, …) and rows are labeled with numbers (1, 2, 3, …), so each cell has an address built from a letter and a number. Cell B2 sits in column B and row 2; cell D17 sits in column D and row 17. The convention is exactly the same as the ordered pair (x, y) from Section 1.4: column letter first, row number second.

A cell can hold either a literal value (a number or a piece of text) or a formula: a small piece of arithmetic that Excel will compute and display. The signal that a cell contains a formula rather than text is the equals sign at the very beginning. The instant Excel sees an = in a cell, it stops treating the contents as text and starts treating them as a calculation.

Definition 1.6. A cell reference is the address (column letter + row number) of a cell, used inside a formula in place of a literal value. A formula is an expression beginning with = that Excel computes and displays, automatically updating whenever any cell it references changes.

Margin noteThe two most common Excel mistakes are forgetting the leading = (which makes the formula display as text) and typing literal numbers where a cell reference belongs (which means edits in the source cell do not propagate). Both are easy to spot once we know to look.
1.6.2  Two built-in functions: =SLOPE and =INTERCEPT

Excel has hundreds of built-in functions, but two of them do exactly what Sections 1.4 and 1.5 asked us to do by hand. The function =SLOPE(y-range, x-range) takes a column of y-values and a column of x-values and returns the slope of the best-fit line through the points. The function =INTERCEPT(y-range, x-range) takes the same two ranges and returns the y-intercept of that line.

Both functions follow the same argument order, which is worth saying out loud once and remembering forever: y-values first, x-values second. Swap the two arguments and Excel will happily compute the slope of x against y, the same numbers but the wrong meaning.

Common mistakeReversing the argument order, writing =SLOPE(B17:B22, C17:C22) when the data are x in column B and y in column C. The result is the slope of time per dollar, not dollars per time. Excel cannot warn us; the numbers look reasonable until we interpret them.
1.6.3  A worked example, end to end

The cleanest way to see the whole workflow is to do it once on a small data set. We will build a linear model for the stopping distance of a car at different speeds, predict a new value, plot the data with a trend line, and interpret the slope and the intercept in real-world terms.

Example 1.6. Stopping distance from data.

A driver-education instructor measures a car's stopping distance at six different speeds and records the results below. We will use Excel to find the linear model d = ms + b, predict the stopping distance at 40 m/s, and decide whether the model can be trusted at very low speeds.

Solution.

Step 1. Enter the data. Speed goes in column B, distance in column C, headers in row 16, data in rows 17 through 22.

Speed (m/s)Distance (m)
108
1522
2030
2546
3055
3570

Step 2. Compute the slope. In an empty cell, type

=SLOPE(C17:C22, B17:B22)

Excel returns m ≈ 2.43. The argument order is y-values first (distance in column C), then x-values (speed in column B).

Step 3. Compute the intercept. In another empty cell, type

=INTERCEPT(C17:C22, B17:B22)

Excel returns b ≈ −16.14. Together the two numbers give the linear model

d = 2.43s − 16.14

Step 4. Predict at a new speed. At 40 m/s (about 90 mph), the model gives

d = 2.43(40) − 16.14 ≈ 81 meters

That is roughly the length of an Olympic swimming pool. In an actual spreadsheet, we would put 40 in a new cell and reference it with a formula like =2.43*E17 − 16.14, so that changing the speed automatically updates the prediction.

Step 5. Plot the data with a trend line. Select the data range (B16:C22), choose Insert → Scatter, pick the X-Y points version (not the connected-line version), right-click a data point, choose Add Trendline, and check Linear and Display Equation on chart. Excel draws the best-fit line through the cloud of points and prints the same equation we computed in Steps 2 and 3. Two paths to the same answer, which is the most useful kind of sanity check.

Step 6. Interpret the numbers in real-world terms. The slope m = 2.43 says that every additional meter-per-second of speed adds about 2.43 meters of stopping distance, which matches our physical intuition: faster cars need more room. The intercept b = −16.14 says that at zero speed the car would stop in −16.14 meters, which makes no physical sense. The intercept here is not a fact about reality; it is a fact about a straight line fitted to data between 10 and 35 m/s. The model is good inside that range, but it should not be trusted at speeds far outside it.
Spreadsheets do the arithmetic. People do the interpreting.
1.6.4  Cell references and drag-to-fill

The reason spreadsheets feel so powerful is that a formula written with cell references updates itself whenever any of those cells changes. If cell B2 contains the value 50 and cell C2 contains =B2*2, then C2 displays 100. Change B2 to 80 and C2 instantly displays 160, with no further work from us.

Cell references also shift when a formula is dragged into new cells. A formula like =A2 + 10 in cell B2, dragged down to B5, becomes =A5 + 10 in row 5. Excel adjusts the row numbers automatically so that each row's formula refers to its own corresponding inputs. That is what lets us write a single formula and apply it to twenty-five rows of data at once.

In the wildFinancial analysts, scientists, sports coaches, healthcare administrators, and small-business owners all use the same handful of moves in Excel: build a model in one row, drag the formula across the data set, change one input, watch every prediction update. The same workflow that found a stopping-distance model can be used to forecast quarterly revenue, predict election outcomes, model patient recovery times, or check whether a meal-prep budget will survive a price hike on chicken.
Exercises

1. A small spreadsheet has the header Week in cell A1 and Savings in cell B1. Rows 2, 3, and 4 contain (0, 200), (1, 275), and (2, 350) respectively. What is the cell address of the cell that contains 350?

    Show answer

    Answer. B4. Column B is the “Savings” column, row 4 is the third data row. Column letter first, row number second, the same convention as (x, y).

  2. A phone plan charges $0.10 per minute plus a $25 base fee. Cell A2 holds the number of minutes used. Write the formula for cell B2 that computes the total bill.

    Show answer

    Answer. =0.10*A2 + 25. The 0.10 multiplies the input cell A2; the 25 is the fixed base fee. This is y = mx + b with a cell reference standing in for x.

  3. Cells A2:A11 hold the year (1 through 10). Cells B2:B11 hold the corresponding home values. Write the Excel formula that returns the slope of the best-fit line.

    Show answer

    Answer. =SLOPE(B2:B11, A2:A11). y-values (home values, column B) come first, x-values (years, column A) come second. Reversing the order would compute years per dollar instead of dollars per year.

  4. Why does every Excel formula begin with =?

    1. It is stylistic; the formula would still work without it.
    2. The = tells Excel to compute the cell instead of displaying its contents as text.
    3. It marks the cell as locked from editing.
    4. It indicates which row the cell is in.
    Show answer

    Answer. B. Without the equals sign, Excel reads the contents as a literal piece of text and shows the characters exactly as typed.

  5. Cell A1 contains the number 50, and cell A2 contains the formula =A1*2. If we change A1 to 80, what happens to A2?

    1. A2 stays at 100; the formula was already evaluated.
    2. A2 shows an error because A1 changed.
    3. A2 automatically updates to 160.
    4. The formula in A2 must be retyped.
    Show answer

    Answer. C. Cell references are live; whenever A1 changes, every formula that depends on A1 is recomputed.

  6. The formula =A2 + 10 is written in cell B2 and dragged down to B5. What formula ends up in cell B5?

    1. =A2 + 10
    2. =B2 + 10
    3. =A2 + 40
    4. =A5 + 10
    Show answer

    Answer. D. Dragging a formula down shifts the row numbers in the cell references: B2 becomes B5, A2 becomes A5. The literal 10 stays put because it is not a reference. That auto-adjustment is exactly why one formula can power twenty-five rows.
Looking ahead

Six sections ago, the word function was a vague label and slope was something to be memorized. We can now read a real-world situation, identify a rate and a starting value, write a linear model, predict from it, sanity-check the prediction, and let a spreadsheet do the heavy lifting at scale. That is the same workflow used in business forecasting, science labs, healthcare planning, sports analytics, and a thousand other places where numbers become decisions. Chapter 2 carries the same habits into the next idea: the language of percent.
1.6
